title: Distributed Systems & Event-Driven
description: Kafka, queues, idempotency, DLQs, exactly-once, CAP.

cards.front: Monolith vs. microservices vs. event-driven — what's the distinction?
cards.back: Monolith: one deployable unit — simple to start but scales as a whole, tightly coupled, single point of failure. Microservices: independently deployable services owning their data — independent scaling, fault isolation, team autonomy, at the cost of distributed complexity. Event-driven: a style where services communicate via events through queues/streams — temporal decoupling, fault isolation, easy fan-out.
distractors: Microservices are one deployable unit; monoliths are many small independent services.
Event-driven means every service calls every other service synchronously over HTTP.
A monolith always scales better than microservices because there are no network calls.
Microservices share a single database and transaction, which is what gives fault isolation.
Event-driven architecture eliminates the need for any queues or message brokers.
Microservices and event-driven are the same thing; the terms are fully interchangeable.
cards.quizAnswer: Monolith: one tightly-coupled unit. Microservices: independent services owning data. Event-driven: services communicate via events for decoupling.

cards.front: Message queue (SQS) vs. event stream (Kafka) — when each?
cards.back: SQS is work distribution: producers enqueue tasks, consumers pull and a message is typically consumed once and removed — for 'one worker must do this task,' with load leveling, retries, DLQs. Kafka is an append-only, durable, replayable, partitioned log where many independent consumers read the same events at their own pace — for 'this happened and many systems may care,' with fan-out and replay.
distractors: SQS retains every message forever for replay; Kafka deletes messages once consumed.
Kafka is best for one-worker task queues; SQS is best for fan-out to many consumers.
Both deliver each message to exactly one consumer and then delete it.
SQS is partitioned for high throughput; Kafka is a simple single-consumer queue.
Kafka cannot have multiple consumers; each event goes to one consumer group only.
SQS guarantees ordering across the whole queue, which Kafka cannot do at all.
cards.quizAnswer: SQS is a pull-based work queue consumed once; Kafka is a durable, replayable, partitioned log read by many consumers.

cards.front: Explain at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once delivery.
cards.back: At-most-once: 0 or 1 deliveries — may be lost, never duplicated (fire-and-forget). At-least-once: 1+ deliveries — never lost, may duplicate (the safe default, what SQS gives). Exactly-once delivery is essentially impossible over an unreliable network; systems achieve exactly-once processing = at-least-once delivery + idempotent consumers (or transactional dedupe).
distractors: At-least-once means messages may be lost but never duplicated.
Exactly-once delivery is trivially guaranteed by any modern message broker.
At-most-once never loses messages; it just may deliver them several times.
Exactly-once processing requires neither idempotency nor at-least-once delivery.
At-least-once is unsafe and should be avoided; at-most-once is the production default.
Exactly-once is achieved by retrying until the consumer crashes, then stopping.
cards.quizAnswer: At-most-once may lose messages; at-least-once may duplicate (the safe default); exactly-once needs idempotent consumers.

cards.front: What is idempotency and how do you implement it?
cards.back: An operation is idempotent if doing it multiple times has the same effect as once — the antidote to at-least-once delivery and retries. Implement with idempotency keys (check before processing, skip duplicates), natural idempotency ('set state to X' not 'increment'), conditional writes/optimistic locking ('update only if state is Y'), or a dedup table with TTL.
distractors: An operation that can only ever be run once and then permanently locks itself.
Implemented by making every operation an 'increment,' which is naturally safe to repeat.
Achieved by retrying an operation until it finally succeeds at least once.
A property of the network that guarantees messages arrive in order.
Implemented by deleting the database row before each write to avoid conflicts.
Encrypting each request so duplicates produce different ciphertext and are rejected.
cards.quizAnswer: An operation whose repeats have the same effect as once; implemented with idempotency keys, conditional writes, or dedup tables.

cards.front: What's a dead-letter queue (DLQ) and why do you need it?
cards.back: A separate queue where messages go after failing processing a set number of times. Without it, a poison message (always fails — bad data or a bug) gets retried forever, blocking the queue. The DLQ isolates these failures: the bad message is parked for inspection/replay while healthy traffic flows. You alert on DLQ depth, debug, fix, and optionally redrive.
distractors: A queue that automatically deletes any message older than 24 hours.
A backup queue that mirrors all messages in case the primary queue is lost.
A high-priority queue where urgent messages skip ahead of normal traffic.
A queue that retries poison messages infinitely until they eventually succeed.
A queue used only during deployments to hold traffic while services restart.
A second consumer that processes the same messages in parallel for speed.
cards.quizAnswer: A queue for messages that fail repeatedly, isolating poison messages so they don't block the queue forever.

cards.front: How do you handle retries safely and avoid retry storms?
cards.back: Exponential backoff + jitter (randomness so clients don't retry in sync and create a thundering herd), bounded retries then DLQ, idempotency as the prerequisite (retries safe only if reprocessing is harmless), a circuit breaker (stop calling a failing downstream for a cooldown, fail fast, then probe), and timeouts on every network call.
distractors: Retry immediately and continuously until the downstream recovers, with no backoff.
Retry all clients on the same fixed interval so the load is predictable.
Remove timeouts so requests wait as long as needed for a slow dependency.
Keep the circuit closed permanently so requests are never blocked.
Retry without idempotency, since backoff alone prevents duplicate effects.
Increase retry counts to unlimited so no message is ever dead-lettered.
cards.quizAnswer: Exponential backoff with jitter, bounded retries to a DLQ, idempotency, circuit breakers, and timeouts on every call.

cards.front: Explain the CAP theorem and where you'd land.
cards.back: During a network Partition (which will happen), you must choose Consistency (every read sees the latest write) or Availability (every request gets a response) — not both. CP systems (relational DB, ZooKeeper) sacrifice availability — use for money/balances/payment state. AP systems (Cassandra, DynamoDB eventual) stay available and reconcile later — use for likes, feeds, catalogs, telemetry. It's per-use-case.
distractors: CAP says you can always have Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance at once.
Partition tolerance is optional; well-built systems simply avoid partitions.
CP systems stay available during partitions; AP systems refuse requests to stay correct.
You should always pick AP, since consistency is never worth losing uptime.
CAP applies only to single-node databases, not to distributed systems.
Choosing Consistency means every request always gets an instant response.
cards.quizAnswer: During a partition you must choose consistency or availability; CP for money, AP for feeds/catalogs. It's per-use-case.

cards.front: Eventual consistency — what is it and when is it acceptable?
cards.back: After a write, replicas converge to the latest value eventually, but a read in the meantime may see stale data — the price of high availability and partition tolerance. Acceptable when a brief stale read causes no harm (social feeds, view counts, catalogs, dashboards, DNS). Not acceptable for read-your-writes-critical or financial-correctness paths.
distractors: Replicas never converge; each holds a permanently different value.
Reads always return the latest write, just after a fixed one-second delay.
It is acceptable for payment balances because the values converge quickly.
It guarantees strong consistency as long as there is no network partition.
It means writes are eventually lost unless re-sent by the client.
It is the strongest consistency model, stronger than ACID transactions.
cards.quizAnswer: Replicas converge to the latest value eventually; a read meanwhile may be stale. Fine when brief staleness is harmless.

cards.front: The dual-write problem and the outbox pattern.
cards.back: Dual-write problem: a service must atomically commit to its DB and publish an event, but they're separate systems with no shared transaction — if one succeeds and the other fails, state and events diverge. Outbox pattern: within the same DB transaction, also insert the event into an outbox table; a relay (or CDC like Debezium) reads the outbox and publishes. The event is published iff the state committed.
distractors: The dual-write problem is writing to two database replicas; solved by picking a leader.
The outbox pattern publishes the event first, then commits the DB if publishing succeeds.
It uses a two-phase commit across the database and broker for true atomicity.
The outbox is an in-memory buffer that flushes events on a timer, losing them on crash.
Dual-write means two services writing the same row; solved with a database lock.
The outbox pattern requires the database and message broker to share one transaction.
cards.quizAnswer: The dual-write problem risks DB and events diverging; the outbox pattern writes the event in the same transaction, then relays it.

cards.front: How does Kafka scale and guarantee ordering?
cards.back: Topics split into partitions distributed across brokers — the horizontal scaling unit (more partitions = more parallelism). Ordering is guaranteed only within a partition, so you pick a partition key (e.g. customer/notification ID) to keep an entity's events ordered together. Consumer groups: each partition is read by one consumer in a group. Replication (leader + followers, acks=all) gives durability.
distractors: Kafka guarantees total ordering across an entire topic regardless of partitions.
Scaling is done by adding consumers beyond the partition count, each reading every partition.
Ordering is guaranteed globally because every event has a timestamp.
Partitions are replicas of each other, so each holds the complete topic for durability.
A partition can be consumed by every consumer in a group simultaneously for speed.
Kafka scales by deleting old partitions and merging their events into one stream.
cards.quizAnswer: Topics split into partitions for scale; ordering holds only within a partition, so choose a partition key per entity.
